segments.title: The Admirals and Lexingtons break the treaty premise
segments.summary: The counterfactual starts by showing why keeping all Admirals and Lexingtons forces a different Washington Naval Treaty settlement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke grounds the scenario in the awkward timing: the Admirals were cancelled before the first Lexington was laid down, so the treaty path has to change before the ships can survive. The key pressure is parity between American Lexingtons and British Admirals, especially because large carriers under the treaty logic need to be conversions. This watch point explains why the scenario cannot simply add ships to the historical treaty and call the rest unchanged.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the real-world cancellation and laying-down dates, then says the Washington Naval Treaty logic must change if these ships exist.
The transcript argues that keeping all six Lexingtons prevents Britain from accepting an inferior large-carrier conversion deal for the Admirals.
The transcript sets up the U.S. desire for six 16-inch-gun ships and pairs three Lexington battlecruisers with three large carriers.

segments.title: Battlecruiser tonnage becomes trade-protection politics
segments.summary: A revised capital-ship settlement uses heavy battlecruisers and trade protection to keep the arms-control deal politically plausible.
segments.body: This section lays out two possible treaty arithmetic choices and rejects the larger total as too weak to count as meaningful arms control. The more plausible version adds limited tonnage for a small number of oversized trade-protection battlecruisers while keeping the fifteen-ship framework recognizable. It also shows why Britain can defend Hood and why Lexington's displacement pushes everyone toward explicit exceptions rather than quiet fudging.

evidence.note: The transcript presents possible 45,000-ton battlecruiser allowances and ties them to trade protection.
The transcript compares Hood and Lexington displacements and explains why a 660,000-ton settlement would not look like serious arms control.
The transcript settles on a more limited framework that lets each navy claim enough of a victory to accept the arrangement.

segments.title: Tosa gives Japan a visible treaty win
segments.summary: Japan's incentive in the altered settlement is completing Tosa, gaining a prestige battleship without making the treaty collapse.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke chooses Tosa because a completed battleship is more useful to Japan in this setup than one isolated battlecruiser. The transcript frames the result as a political and naval improvement for Japan: a larger special-ship position, one completed 39,000-ton battleship, and a settlement that may feel better than the historical Washington outcome. This is the key Japan-focused watch point before the video moves into carrier categories.

evidence.note: The transcript says Japan gets Tosa completed because that battleship is more useful than another battlecruiser in this scenario.
The transcript argues Japan can claim a win, improve its special-ship position, and leave this treaty happier than the historical Washington settlement.
The transcript notes Tosa's 39,000-ton standard displacement and suggests the other powers could accept one Japanese battleship.

segments.title: A strike-carrier category solves the conversion problem
segments.summary: The carrier side of the counterfactual creates a larger conversion-only strike-carrier category alongside smaller standard carriers.
segments.body: The historical treaty permitted only two unusually large carrier conversions, but the question needs three large conversions for both Britain and the United States. Dr. Clarke's solution is a special strike-carrier category, with Britain and the U.S. receiving three large conversions and Japan two, while ordinary carriers remain smaller. The segment is useful for following how tonnage rules shape carrier size, national quotas, and whether Courageous, Glorious, or later purpose-built carriers still make sense.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the historical carrier limits with the need for three large conversions and names a strike-carrier category.
The transcript sketches special conversion limits for Britain, the United States, and Japan, plus larger total carrier allowances.
The transcript reasons through ordinary-carrier tonnage so new carriers are not too weak compared with converted strike carriers.

segments.title: Six Lexington hulls become a logistics advantage
segments.summary: The U.S. gains a common Lexington machinery pool, but the paired battlecruiser-and-carrier force complicates carrier armament choices.
segments.body: For the U.S. Navy, three Lexington battlecruisers and three related carriers create a support system with common power plants, engineering training, manuals, and spare parts. That commonality is a major operational payoff of the counterfactual. The same pairing raises a design question: if a carrier normally works with a 16-inch-gun battlecruiser, heavy 8-inch carrier guns become less obviously necessary, even though extra displacement may let designers keep them.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the U.S. logistics benefit of six ships with common machinery, training manuals, engineers, and spares.
The transcript connects Lexington carriers with paired 16-inch-gun battlecruisers and questions the need for heavy carrier guns.
The transcript notes the larger displacement margin and concludes the carriers may still keep heavy guns despite the escort logic.

segments.title: Britain turns Admirals into carriers and keeps G3 logic
segments.summary: The Royal Navy's cleanest path is three Admiral-class carriers plus new fast capital ships rather than a mixed one-off carrier fleet.
segments.body: This section works through the British conversion dilemma. Admirals are long and infrastructure-heavy, but making all three remaining ships carriers gives the Royal Navy a standardized strike-carrier group instead of a patchwork of one-off hulls. Dr. Clarke argues that the G3/F3 line remains more attractive for new fast capital ships, while smaller carriers such as Courageous and Glorious could still fill fleet-carrier roles below the strike-carrier tier.

evidence.note: The transcript poses the British conversion choice between Admirals and G3s, then discusses flight-deck layout and strike-carrier purpose.
The transcript explains why Courageous and Glorious might still be converted as smaller fleet carriers under the revised limits.
The transcript argues against mixed Admiral/G3 carrier conversions and favors three standardized Admiral-class carriers plus new battlecruisers.

segments.title: Early fast task forces reshape naval aviation
segments.summary: British strike carriers sailing with battlecruisers create an early fast task-force model and could change Fleet Air Arm organization.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke imagines a British world cruise with Hood, Repulse, and large Admiral-derived carriers as a diplomatic and military signal. That force becomes an early version of the fast carrier task force: carriers and battlecruisers operating together rather than as separate prestige assets. The large air groups also matter institutionally because 90-aircraft carriers make squadron organization, fighter demand, torpedo-bomber strength, and naval control of aircraft procurement more consequential.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Tosa, British carrier command choices, battlecruisers sailing with carriers, and an early fast task-force form.
The transcript estimates large carrier air groups and links them to Fleet Air Arm squadron structure.
The transcript sketches reconnaissance, torpedo-bomber, and fighter mixes and says fighter demand and procurement control could change.

segments.title: Carrier-battlecruiser pairing changes World War II habits
segments.summary: By World War II, practiced carrier-and-fast-capital-ship teams could alter early strikes, escort doctrine, and the battlecruiser's reputation.
segments.body: The later counterfactual payoff is operational rather than just numerical. Dr. Clarke argues that navies with fifteen years of large strike-carrier practice would distribute forces differently, try different early-war carrier strikes, and treat fast capital ships as normal carrier escorts. That pairing could affect episodes such as German-base strikes, Glorious's loss, Taranto, and Pacific carrier groupings, while also making battlecruisers look like useful carrier guardians rather than obsolete relics.

evidence.note: The transcript places strike carriers and battlecruisers around the Atlantic, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, then considers early strikes on German naval bases.
The transcript discusses Pacific task forces, fast oilers, battlecruisers as carrier escorts, and the battlecruiser's changed wartime legacy.
The transcript applies carrier-battlecruiser pairing to Glorious, multi-carrier operations, Taranto, and possible Anglo-American carrier links in the Pacific.
